questions.question: Explain Secure Shell (SSH) and its primary functions.
keyPoints: SSH is a cryptographic network protocol for secure communication over unsecured networks
Provides secure remote login replacing Telnet/rsh
Enables secure file transfer (SCP/SFTP) and port forwarding
Uses client-server architecture with encryption

questions.question: List and explain three SSH authentication methods.
keyPoints: Password authentication: Traditional method with encrypted password transfer
Public key authentication: Uses key pairs (private/public) for passwordless login
Keyboard-interactive: Multi-step authentication similar to password

questions.question: What is SSH port forwarding? Explain local port forwarding with syntax.
keyPoints: Port forwarding tunnels insecure traffic through encrypted SSH connection
Local port forwarding (-L): forwards local port through SSH to remote destination
Syntax: ssh -L local_port:remote_host:remote_port user@server
Example: ssh -L 8080:webserver:80 user@gateway accesses webserver:80 via gateway

questions.question: Differentiate between SCP and SFTP protocols.
keyPoints: SCP is simpler, uses single connection for file transfer
SFTP is a subsystem of SSH with FTP-like interface
SFTP supports more operations: ls, cd, mkdir, rm, rename
SFTP provides better error handling and file attribute support

questions.question: Explain the SSH-1 vs SSH-2 protocol versions and why SSH-2 is preferred.
keyPoints: SSH-1 has vulnerabilities (CRC32 exploit) and uses single stream
SSH-2 has improved security with stronger encryption algorithms
SSH-2 supports key exchange algorithms and better modular architecture
SSH-2 provides forward secrecy and resistance to attacks

questions.question: Describe the purpose and usage of SSH agent and SSH keys.
keyPoints: ssh-agent stores decrypted private keys in memory
Eliminates repeated passphrase entry for authentication
Keys: RSA, DSA (deprecated), ECDSA, Ed25519 (recommended)
ssh-keygen generates key pairs, ssh-add adds keys to agent

questions.question: List and explain SSH configuration files and their purposes.
keyPoints: System-wide: /etc/ssh/sshd_config (server), /etc/ssh/ssh_config (client)
User-specific: ~/.ssh/config for client options
~/.ssh/authorized_keys for public key authentication
~/.ssh/known_hosts stores server keys for verification

questions.question: Explain the SSH architecture and working principle. Include discussion on encryption, key exchange, and session establishment.
keyPoints: SSH uses client-server model with sshd daemon on server
Connection involves: TCP handshake, protocol version exchange, key exchange (Diffie-Hellman)
Session encryption established using generated session keys
Uses both asymmetric (for auth/key exchange) and symmetric encryption (for data)
Authentication methods verify user identity
Final step: session channel opened for secure communication

questions.question: Explain in detail the three types of SSH port forwarding with examples and use cases.
keyPoints: Local (-L): Forwards local port through SSH to remote destination. Example: ssh -L 8080:internal:80 gateway - accesses internal:80 via gateway
Remote (-R): Forwards remote port to local machine. Example: ssh -R 8080:localhost:80 remote - remote can access local web server
Dynamic (-D): Creates SOCKS proxy. Example: ssh -D 9090 user@server - use localhost:9090 as proxy for any destination
Use cases: Accessing firewalled services, secure browsing, tunneling applications

questions.question: Explain the process of passwordless SSH login using public key authentication with necessary commands.
keyPoints: Generate key pair: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 (or rsa)
Key pair: private key (~/.ssh/id_ed25519) stays local, public key (.pub) goes to server
Copy public key: ssh-copy-id user@server or manually append to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
Server stores public key in authorized_keys file
On connection: client signs with private key, server verifies with stored public key
Optional: Add passphrase and use ssh-agent to avoid repeated passphrase entry

questions.question: Discuss SSH security best practices and common SSH commands used in system administration.
keyPoints: Best practices: Use SSH-2 only, disable root login, use key-based auth, change default port, use strong key types (Ed25519)
Limit user access, use AllowUsers/DenyUsers, implement fail2ban, keep SSH updated
Common commands: ssh (login), scp/sftp (transfer), ssh-keygen (keys)
ssh-copy-id (deploy keys), ssh-agent (key management)
ssh-add (add keys), sshuttle (VPN-like tunnel)
Configuration: /etc/ssh/sshd_config modifications for hardening
